coredata.dc:title: Multi-branched gold nanoflower-embedded iron porphyrin for colorimetric immunosensor
coredata.dc:creator: Zhou, Yaofeng
Huang, Xiaolin
Zhang, Wenjing
Ji, Yanwei
Chen, Rui
Xiong, Yonghua
coredata.dc:description: Here, we for the first time used a thiolated amino-ligand modified multi-branched gold nanoflower as skeleton to encapsulate iron porphyrins (AuNF@FeTPPCl) as alternatives to horseradish peroxidase (HRP). After the FeTPPCl encapsulation, the HRP mimicking activity of FeTPPCl was effectively blocked by the steric hindrance of the hydrophobic layer on the AuNF surface. Upon the addition of ethanol, the loaded FeTPPCl was released into the solution in its free format and exposed the catalytic sites, resulting in the recovery of catalytic activity. The proposed encapsulation method effectively avoided the loss of catalytic activity that originated from the blocking of the catalytic active sites during immobilization. Additionally, the resultant AuNF@FeTPPCl nanocomposite exhibited a high loading level with 2.4 × 106 FeTPPCl molecules per AuNF, and showed considerably high catalytic activity for hydrogen peroxide and 3, 3′ 5, 5′-tetramethylbenzidine, which is approximately 40- and 172-folds higher than native HRP. Through using the as-prepared AuNF@FeTPPCl as an alternative of HRP for trace labeling, we successfully developed colorimetric immunosensors for fumonisin B1 (FB1) and hepatitis B surface antigen (HBsAg) with competitive and sandwich-type formats, respectively. The developed AuNF@FeTPPCl-based colorimetric immunosensor exhibited higher detection sensitivity for FB1 and HBsAg than the corresponding HRP-based immunosensors. Thus, the proposed AuNF@FeTPPCl can be used as HRP mimicking analogs for developing highly sensitive colorimetric immunosensor and for loading other hydrophobic iron porphyrins or catalysts.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Iron porphyrin
Multi-branched gold nanoflower
Peroxidase mimic
Colorimetric immunosensors

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast footnotes grantnumber grantsponsor highlightsabst primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Encapsulation of FeTPPCl into amino-ligand-modified AuNFs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of the prepared AuNFs (Inset: AuNF@FeTPPCl). (B) EDS spectra of AuNF@FeTPPCl. (C) UV–vis absorption spectra of AuNF and AuNF@FeTPPCl. (D) Photo of color variations and UV–vis absorption spectra of TMB reaction under different conditions. (a) TMB, (b) H2O2+TMB, (c) FeTPPCl+H2O2+TMB, and (e) AuNF@FeTPPCl+H2O2+TMB, were performed in 0.2M acetate buffer containing 70% ethanol (pH 2.0); (d) AuNF@FeTPPCl+H2O2+TMB was performed in 0.2M acetate buffer (pH 2.0) without ethanol. The concentrations of FeTPPCl, AuNF@FeTPPCl, H2O2 and TMB were 1.6nM, 0.8 fM, 0.5M, and 2mM, respectively. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.).

(A) The effect of ethanol concentration on the release efficiency of FeTPPCl. The effects of pH (B), temperature (C), and H2O2 concentration (D) on the peroxidase-like activity of AuNF@FeTPPCl (after release) and HRP.

Comparison of stability of AuNF@FeTPPCl (black line) and HRP (red line) towards the relative catalytic activity after incubation at different temperatures (A), pH values (B), NaCl concentrations (C) for 2h, storage stability (D) in 4°C, and the activity was measured under optimum conditions. For each curve, the maximum point was set at 100%, and the relative activities for other absorptions were calculated accordingly. Error bars indicate the standard deviations of three independent measurements. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.).

Photographs and calibration curves of the proposed AuNF@FeTPPCl based colorimetric immunosensor relative to target standards with various concentrations: (A, B) FB1, and (C, D) HBsAg. Vertical bars indicate the standard deviation (n = 3).
Schematic illustration of (A) AuNF@FeTPPCl preparation, and (B) colorimetric immunosensor fabrication through using AuNF@FeTPPCl as trace label.
Comparison of kinetic parameters of HRP, AuNF@FeTPPCl, and FeTPPCl.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: The concentration of AuNF@FeTPPCl, FeTPPCl and HRP are 0.5pM, 714nM and 15.3pM, respectively.

head.ce:title: Multi-branched gold nanoflower-embedded iron porphyrin for colorimetric immunosensor

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Food Science and Technology, Nanchang University, Nanchang 330047, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Food Science and Technology, Nanchang University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangxi-OAI Joint Research Institute, Nanchang University, Nanchang 330047, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangxi-OAI Joint Research Institute, Nanchang University

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence to: State Key Laboratory of Food Science and Technology, and Jiangxi-OAI Joint Research Institute, Nanchang University, 235 Nanjing East Road, Nanchang 330047, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Food Science and Technology, and Jiangxi-OAI Joint Research Institute, Nanchang University

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Here, we for the first time used a thiolated amino-ligand modified multi-branched gold nanoflower as skeleton to encapsulate iron porphyrins (AuNF@FeTPPCl) as alternatives to horseradish peroxidase (HRP). After the FeTPPCl encapsulation, the HRP mimicking activity of FeTPPCl was effectively blocked by the steric hindrance of the hydrophobic layer on the AuNF surface. Upon the addition of ethanol, the loaded FeTPPCl was released into the solution in its free format and exposed the catalytic sites, resulting in the recovery of catalytic activity. The proposed encapsulation method effectively avoided the loss of catalytic activity that originated from the blocking of the catalytic active sites during immobilization. Additionally, the resultant AuNF@FeTPPCl nanocomposite exhibited a high loading level with 2.4 × 106 FeTPPCl molecules per AuNF, and showed considerably high catalytic activity for hydrogen peroxide and 3, 3′ 5, 5′-tetramethylbenzidine, which is approximately 40- and 172-folds higher than native HRP. Through using the as-prepared AuNF@FeTPPCl as an alternative of HRP for trace labeling, we successfully developed colorimetric immunosensors for fumonisin B1 (FB1) and hepatitis B surface antigen (HBsAg) with competitive and sandwich-type formats, respectively. The developed AuNF@FeTPPCl-based colorimetric immunosensor exhibited higher detection sensitivity for FB1 and HBsAg than the corresponding HRP-based immunosensors. Thus, the proposed AuNF@FeTPPCl can be used as HRP mimicking analogs for developing highly sensitive colorimetric immunosensor and for loading other hydrophobic iron porphyrins or catalysts.

ce:section.ce:para: Colorimetric immunosensor is an important analytical platform characterized by visualization, simplicity, low cost, and high throughput and is widely used in clinical diagnosis, environmental monitoring, and food quality control (Chang et al., 2017; Huang et al., 2016a; Xiong et al., 2017b). Horseradish peroxidase (HRP) is used as enzyme label to catalyze the colorless peroxidase substrate 3,3,5,5-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) to yield blue colored products as signal outputs (Gong et al., 2017; Zhao et al., 2016; Zou et al., 2016). HRP has been increasingly used to develop colorimetric immunosensor because of its high catalytic efficiency under mild conditions (Li et al., 2016a; Ravan, 2016; Xiong et al., 2017a). However, as a natural enzyme, HRP exhibits catalytic activity that is highly dependent on protein conformation, leading to vulnerability to external conditions; this property limits the practical application of HRP (Corma, 2016). Thus, designing HRP mimic analogs as stable alternatives is important and remains a challenging topic (Lin et al., 2014).

With the rapid development of nanoscience and nanotechnology, nanomaterials have been increasingly used to develop HRP mimics with excellent activity (Han et al., 2015); such materials include Fe3O4 nanoparticles (Wang et al., 2017), gold nanomaterials (Jiang et al., 2015), silver nanoparticles (Ju et al., 2016), carbon nanomaterials (Sun et al., 2015), polypyrrole nanoparticles (Periasamy et al., 2016), TiO2 nanotube (Zhao et al., 2015), CeO2 nanoparticles (Tian et al., 2017), Co3O4 nanoparticles (Huang et al., 2016b), V2O5 nanowires (Mu et al., 2014), CdS nanoparticles (Liu et al., 2015b), and hybrid nanostructures (Singh et al., 2017). Nanomaterial-based enzymes (nanozymes) are less expensive and have higher chemical stability than natural enzymes (Jiang et al., 2015; Sun et al., 2015). Despite progress achieved in the field of nanozymes, controllable and large-scale synthesis of these materials remains challenging in biosensing application. On one hand, the intrinsic HRP mimicking activity of nanozymes is too weak to be applied to ultrasensitive biosensing (Cheng et al., 2016; Nagvenkar and Gedanken, 2016). On the other hand, the catalytic activity of nanozymes strictly depends on the nanomaterial surface; as such, additional coating and bioconjugation will significantly reduce their catalytic activity (Xia et al., 2015).

Previous studies demonstrated that the catalytic centers of HRP consist of iron-containing porphyrin compounds (Campomanes et al., 2015; Dong et al., 2015). Various iron porphyrins, including hemin and iron(III) meso-tetrakis(N-methylpyridinum-4-yl)porphyrin (FeTMPyP), are employed to mimic HRP bioactivity (Liu et al., 2017; Zhang et al., 2017). Nevertheless, free iron porphyrins have weaker mimicking peroxidase activity relative to HRP and thus cannot be integrated into conventional immunoassay platform (Liu et al., 2015a; Wang et al., 2015). To improve the catalytic performance for biosensing application, scholars design suitable scaffolds for loading of iron porphyrin molecules. Reported supported scaffolds include G-quadruplex (Wang et al., 2014a), supramolecular hydrogels (Qu et al., 2015), graphene (He et al., 2014), metal organic frameworks (Zhou et al., 2017), and covalent organic frameworks (Wang et al., 2014b). Although these strategies can significantly enhance the catalytic activity of iron porphyrin catalysts, they exhibit certain limitations. These iron porphyrin molecules are conjugated onto the supported surface through physical adsorption and covalent coupling, leading to inadequate contact between the enzyme and the substrate (Deng et al., 2013), decreased activity from porphyrin axial ligand saturation, and enzyme detachment (Deng et al., 2017). Moreover, the immobilization of iron porphyrins onto the supported surface blocks the catalytic active centers (Luo et al., 2015), rendering the site inaccessible to the substrate and eventually reducing the HRP mimicking activity. To overcome the limitations of existing HRP mimics, scholars have developed a new kind of three-dimensional amorphous covalent organic polymers (COPs) as skeleton for immobilization or encapsulation of iron porphyrins. For example, Jin et al. (2017) designed and synthesized porphyrin-based COPs (FePor-TFPA-COP) through the aromatic substitution reaction of pyrrole and tris(4-formylphenyl)amine (TFPA). The fabricated FePor-TFPA-COP possesses a large surface area and abundant surface catalytic active sites, resulting in significantly increased HRP mimicking activity. However, similar to other enzyme mimicking analogs, the covalently immobilized iron porphyrins from FePor-TFPA-COP partially block the catalytic active sites to prevent the access of the substrate, thereby reducing the catalytic activity. Moreover, the synthesis of FePor-TFPA-COP is complicated and time consuming. Therefore, a simple scaffold for loading of iron porphyrins without compromising HRP mimicking activity must be designed.

In this study, we designed and synthesized multi-branched gold nanoflowers (AuNFs) modified with thiolated amino ligand as skeleton to encapsulate iron porphyrins. The thiolated amino ligand consists of hydrophobic alkane chain for iron porphyrin encapsulation, a tetra(ethylene glycol) unit for biocompatibility and solubility, and a functional terminal amino group for functionalization with biorecognition molecules to target specific analytes for biosensing. To demonstrate the feasibility of the design, we selected the hydrophobic 5,10,15,20-tetraphenyl-21H,23H-porphyrin iron(III) chloride (FeTPPCl) as an iron porphyrin model to catalyze TMB oxidation with H2O2. The encapsulation of FeTPPCl onto the AuNF surface formed AuNF@FeTPPCl complex through hydrophobic interactions between the alkane chain and FeTPPCl. The encapsulation of FeTPPCl into the hydrophobic layer blocked the access to the catalytic sites because of steric hindrance (Yang et al., 2013), thereby completely inhibiting the catalytic activity (activity “off”). Interestingly, the addition of the organic solvent destructed the hydrophobic interaction between the alkane chain and FeTPPCl, triggering the release of the latter. Subsequently, the blocked catalytic site was exposed, and the activity recovered (activity “on”). Compared with previously reported scaffolds, the proposed thiolated amino-ligand modified AuNFs exhibited significantly higher loading of FeTPPCl and organic solvent-responsive property. The released FeTPPCl also catalyzed substrate oxidation in its free form, thereby effectively avoiding the loss of catalytic activity from the blockage of the catalytic active sites during immobilization ( Scheme 1 A). Through combining conventional immunoassays, the resultant AuNF@FeTPPCl nanocomposites as trace label was used to construct colorimetric immunosensor through competitive and sandwich formats for fumonisin B1 (FB1) and hepatitis B surface antigen (HBsAg) detection, respectively. The half-maximal inhibitory concentration (IC50) for FB1 and the limit of detection (LOD) for HBsAg of our proposed methods were achieved at 101pg/mL and 7.8pg/mL, which are 247- and 19.5-folds lower than those of corresponding HRP-based immunosensors, respectively (Scheme 1 B). In summary, the thiolated amino-ligand modified AuNFs exhibit potential as skeleton for FeTPPCl encapsulation and for loading of other hydrophobic iron porphyrins or catalysts, and the designed AuNF@FeTPPCl can be used as HRP mimicking analogs for the fabrication of highly sensitive colorimetric immunosensors.

Bovine serum albumin (BSA), protein G, streptavidin, FB1, TMB, H2O2, HAuCl4·3H2O, sodium citrate, hydroquinone, FeTPPCl, hydroxy-2,5-dioxopyrrolidine-3-sulfonic acid sodium salt (Sulfo-NHS), and 1-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC·HCl) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Chemical Co. (St. Louis, Mo, USA). Anti-FB1 monoclonal antibodies (anti-FB1-mAbs) were presented by prof. Yang Xu of Nanchang University (Nanchang, China). Human hepatitis B surface antigen (HBsAg) was purchased from LincBio Science Co. (Shanghai, China). Anti-human HBsAg monoclonal antibodies (anti-HBsAg mAbs, 6.4 and 3.2mg/mL, respectively) were obtained from Beijing Hotgen BioTech (Beijing, China) and used as capture and detection antibodies, respectively. Biotinylated detected anti-HBsAg mAbs were prepared with biotin N-hydroxysuccinimide ester according to the manufacturer instructions (Sigma–Aldrich Chemical Co.). The 96-well microplates were obtained from Corning Inc. (New York, USA). Ultrapure water was prepared by Elix-3 and Milli-QA (Molsheim, France). All the other chemical reagents were of analytical grade and purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Corp. (Shanghai, China). All chemicals and materials were used without further purification.

AuNFs with an average diameter of 90nm were prepared through seed-mediated growth method according to our previous report (Li et al., 2016b). Colloidal gold seed solutions, with particle diameter of 20nm, were synthesized through classical hydrothermal reduction with citrate. In brief, 1mL of 1% (w/v) HAuCl4 solution was added to 99mL of ultrapure water, and the mixture was heated to boiling. The boiled solution was quickly added with 2.7mL of 1% (w/v) sodium citrate solution under vigorous stirring and boiled again for 10min. The resulting colloidal gold seed solutions were cooled at room temperature for subsequent use. AuNFs were prepared using hydroquinone synthesis method (Xu et al., 2016). Briefly, 375μL of 1% (w/v) HAuCl4, 220μL of 1% sodium citrate, and 1.78mL of 20nm colloidal gold seed solutions were added to 100mL of ultrapure water under vigorous stirring. The mixture was then added with 1mL of 30mM hydroquinone solution and incubated at ambient temperature for another 30min to obtain 90nm AuNFs. The prepared AuNFs were stored at 4°C for further use.

Amino ligand-modified AuNFs were prepared through ligand exchange method (Smith et al., 2015). In brief, 20mL of the as-prepared AuNFs (1nM) were concentrated with centrifugation at 2400g for 15min. The supernatant was removed, and the pellets were resuspended in 1mL of ultrapure water containing excess thiolated ligand (200µg). The ligand exchange reaction was performed at room temperature with constant stirring. After reaction for 10h, the mixture was centrifuged at 2400g for 15min, and the precipitates were washed three times with ultrapure water. The resulting amino-ligand-modified AuNFs were resuspended in ultrapure water to a final concentration of 1nM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Encapsulation of FeTPPCl into amino-ligand-modified AuNFs
ce:section.ce:para: FeTPPCl (3mg) was dissolved in 1mL of tetrahydrofuran (THF) to prepare AuNF@FeTPPCl composite. The prepared FeTPPCl solution was mixed with 1mL of the amino ligand-modified AuNF solution (1nM). After well mixing, the THF was slowly removed through evaporation. During the process, hydrophobic FeTPPCl was slowly encapsulated in the hydrophobic layer of AuNFs to form AuNF@FeTPPCl. After complete evaporation of THF, the resultant AuNF@FeTPPCl was purified by centrifugation at 2400g for 15min. The precipitates were washed and resuspended in ultrapure water at a final concentration of 1nM. The obtained AuNF@FeTPPCl solution was stored at 4°C for further use.

The BSA molecules were covalently conjugated onto the AuNF surface through the formation of peptide bond between the carboxyl group of BSA and the amino group of AuNF@FeTPPCl in the presence of EDC. BSA (10mg/mL, 100μL) was added to 1mL of AuNF@FeTPPCl (1nM), and the mixture was stirred at room temperature for 30min. The mixture was then added with 50μL of EDC (0.1mg/mL) three times for every 30min. The mixture was centrifuged at 2400g for 15min to remove excess BSA. The precipitates of AuNF@FeTPPCl@BSA were washed twice and suspended in ultrapure water. The suspension was stored at 4°C until further use.

FB1 was activated for 15min at room temperature by adding 1mg of FB1 into 0.6mL of 0.1M (pH 4.7) MES buffer solution including 0.9M NaCl and 1mg of EDC according to a previously reported method (Venkataramana et al., 2014). The EDC-activated FB1 solution was added to 1mL of AuNF@FeTPPCl@BSA (1nM), and the mixture was allowed to react at room temperature for 2h. The reaction mixture was centrifuged at 2400g for 15min to remove excess unreacted FB1. Finally, the precipitates of AuNF@FeTPPCl@BSA@FB1 were washed by ultrapure water and re-suspended in 0.01M PB (pH 7.4). The obtained AuNF@FeTPPCl@BSA@FB1 conjugates were used as competing antigen for competitive immunosensor.

SA and AuNF@FeTPPCl conjugates were prepared as enzyme label for sandwich immunosensor. Briefly, 70µg of SA was added to 1mL of the AuNF@FeTPPCl solution (1nM) in the presence of 0.1mg of EDC. The mixture was stirred for 30min at room temperature. The AuNF@FeTPPCl@SA conjugates were washed, collected through centrifugation, and resuspended in 0.01M PB (pH 7.4).

The 96-well microplates were coated with 100μL of protein G (20μg/mL) in bicarbonate buffer (100mM, pH 8.6) at 4°C overnight. The microplates were washed three times with washing buffer (PBS, pH 7.4, 0.01M, containing 0.05% Tween 20) and blocked with 340μL of blocking buffer (1mg/mL of BSA in PBS) for 2h at 37°C. After washing three times with washing buffer, each well of the microplates was added with 100μL of anti-FB1-mAbs diluted to 0.2μg/mL in PBS. The plates were incubated at 37°C for 2h and washed three times with washing buffer. Each well was then added with 50μL of AuNF@FeTPPCl@BSA@FB1 conjugates (250pM) with a fourfold dilution in 0.02M (pH 7.0) PB containing 5mM NaCl. Subsequently, 50μL of FB1 standards or sample solutions were added per well. After incubation at 37°C for 30min, the microplates were washed three times with washing buffer and twice with PB. Each well was then added with 100μL of 0.2M acetate buffer containing 70% ethanol and 100μL of TMB (2mM) and H2O2 (0.5M) substrate solution and incubated. After reaction for 10min, the absorbance at 652nm was recorded with a microplate reader, and photographs of the solution were taken with a Sony DSC-HX300 digital camera. For comparison, a HRP-based conventional immunosensor was developed.

The 96-well microplates were modified with 100μL of captured anti-HBsAg mAb (5μg/mL) in PBS (pH 8.6) per well at 4°C overnight. After washing three times with washing buffer, the microplates were blocked with blocking buffer at 37°C for 2h and washed three times with washing buffer. Each well was added with 100μL of HBsAg standards or sample solutions with desired concentrations. After incubation at 37°C for 1h, the microplates were washed three times with washing buffer. Each well was then added with 100μL of biotinylated detected anti-HBsAg mAb (5μg/mL) and incubated at 37°C for 1h. After washing the plates three times with washing buffer, 100μL of AuNF@FeTPPCl@SA (250pM) was added per well. After incubation for 30min at 37°C, the microplates were washed three times with washing buffer and twice with PB. Subsequently, 100μL of 0.2M acetate buffer containing 70% ethanol and 100μL of TMB (2mM) and H2O2 (0.5M) substrate solution were added per well and allowed to react for 10min. Absorbance at 652nm was recorded with a microplate reader, and photographs of the solution were taken with a Sony DSC-HX300 digital camera. For comparison, a HRP-based conventional immunosensor was developed.

The multi-branched AuNFs were synthesized by using seed-mediated growth method. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image ( Fig. 1 A) shows the resultant AuNFs possess a flower-like structure with average size of 90 ± 6nm (n = 50). The maximum absorption peak of AuNFs is observed at 645nm (Fig. 1 C). Dynamic light scattering (DLS) analysis reveals the average hydrodynamic diameter (DH) of AuNFs is 109nm (Fig. S1). The thiolated amino-ligand was synthesized and conjugated onto the AuNF surface by ligand exchange method (see Supporting information). After amino-ligand coupling, the zeta potential of resultant AuNFs significantly increases from −26.6 to 34.9mV, indicating the thiolated amino-ligand was successfully conjugated onto the AuNF surface through Au-S bond. The FeTPPCl was encapsulated in the hydrophobic layer of AuNFs to form AuNF@FeTPPCl through hydrophobic interaction. The TEM image of AuNF@FeTPPCl indicates no obvious aggregation, morphological changes or decomposition (inset of Fig. 1 A), which was further confirmed by DLS analysis (Fig. S1). Energy-dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) analysis (Fig. 1 B) demonstrates the presence of Au and Fe in the AuNF@FeTPPCl complex. Additionally, Fig. 1 C shows an obvious blue shift in the maximum absorption peak of AuNFs after FeTPPCl loading because the FeTPPCl changed the dielectric constant on the AuNF surface. These findings confirm the successful preparation of AuNF@FeTPPCl nanocomposites. The loading amounts of FeTPPCl catalyst were quantified. Result in Fig. S2 shows that AuNF@FeTPPCl displays a high catalyst loading level with 2.4 × 106 FeTPPCl molecules per AuNF (the detailed calculation was shown in Supporting information). The HRP mimicking activity of AuNF@FeTPPCl was evaluated through the catalytic oxidation of TMB with/without ethanol. As shown in Fig. 1 D, both TMB solution and TMB/H2O2 mixture appears colorless (curve a and b). Upon direct incubation of AuNF@FeTPPCl with TMB/H2O2 mixture without ethanol, the color of the solution is unchanged (curve d). For comparison, free FeTPPCl generates a typical blue-colored solution with maximum absorption at 652nm (curve c). This finding suggests that the HRP mimicking activity of AuNF@FeTPPCl was completely blocked after FeTPPCl encapsulation. This phenomenon could be due to the fact that the hydrophobic layer effectively hinders the access of substrates to the catalytic active sites because of the steric hindrance effect. Interestingly, the addition of high ethanol concentration (70%) could destroy the hydrophobic interaction between the alkane chain and FeTPPCl, inducing the release of the catalyst from the AuNF surface. Similar to free FeTPPCl, the released FeTPPCl can accelerate the production of blue-colored solution through one-electron oxidation of TMB (Fig. 1 D, curve e). Hence, the HRP mimicking activity can be recovered by ethanol-induced release. Furthermore, we also studied the influence of ethanol concentration on the release efficiency of FeTPPCl. Fig. 2 A shows that FeTPPCl was not released when the ethanol concentration is less than 70% (v/v). With increasing ethanol concentration to 70%, an obvious blue-green solution is produced because the FeTPPCl release triggers the TMB catalytic oxidation, while no significant difference in the solution color and absorbance at 652nm is observed with further increasing ethanol concentration. Thus, 70% ethanol is sufficient to ensure the complete release of FeTPPCl from the AuNF surface. In addition, the catalytic activity of the released FeTPPCl did not significantly decrease compared with original free FeTPPCl at the same molar concentration (Fig. S3), indicating the encapsulation and release of FeTPPCl from the AuNF surface does not alter its catalytic activity.

To obtain the highest catalytic activity of the resultant AuNF@FeTPPCl, we optimized the catalytic reaction conditions, including reaction pH, temperature, and H2O2 concentration. To elucidate the difference between the prepared AuNF@FeTPPCl and native enzymes, we used HRP for comparison. Fig. 2 B indicates that the catalytic activity of AuNF@FeTPPCl, similar to HRP, is pH dependent, and the optimal pH was set at 2. Fig. 2 C shows that the catalytic activity of HRP exhibits a strict temperature-dependence, with a narrow optimal temperature range, while AuNF@FeTPPCl maintains a high catalytic activity within the given temperature range, indicating the higher stability of AuNF@FeTPPCl to temperature change compared with HRP. Moreover, we find that the catalytic activity of HRP is dependent on H2O2 concentration. The high H2O2 concentration can lead to the complete loss of the catalytic activity of HRP because of the destruction of the protein structure at high H2O2 concentration. By contrast, AuNF@FeTPPCl shows higher endurance against high H2O2 concentration than HRP, and the highest catalytic activity is obtained by using 500mM H2O2 (Fig. 2 D). These results imply the robustness of the developed AuNF@FeTPPCl as HRP mimicking analog under harsh reaction conditions.

Under the optimal reaction conditions, the catalytic properties of HRP, FeTPPCl, and AuNF@FeTPPCl were estimated by steady-state kinetics assay (Liu et al., 2016). The tested parameters include K m values (Michaelis–Menten constant), V max values (maximum reaction rate), and catalytic constant (K cat, which represents the maximum number of product formation per catalyst per second). Typical Michaelis–Menten equations were obtained by changing H2O2 or TMB concentrations (Fig. S4), and the results are summarized in Table 1. Using H2O2 as substrates, the K cat of AuNF@FeTPPCl is 1.31 × 105 s−1, which is approximately 40- and 1.49 × 106-folds higher than HRP and FeTPPCl, respectively. Using TMB as substrates, the K cat of AuNF@FeTPPCl is 3.92 × 105 s−1, which is approximately 172- and 1.4 × 106-folds higher than HRP and FeTPPCl, respectively. These results imply the AuNF@FeTPPCl exhibits higher catalytic efficiency for H2O2 and TMB relative to HRP and original FeTPPCl because of a high loading of FeTPPCl onto the AuNF surface. In comparison with the original FeTPPCl, AuNF@FeTPPCl shows similar K m and V max values, indicating that the encapsulation and release does not affect the catalytic performance of FeTPPCl.

We investigated the environmental tolerance of AuNF@FeTPPCl and HRP through 2h of treatment under different pH values, environment temperatures, and NaCl concentrations. The catalytic activities of AuNF@FeTPPCl and HRP were monitored through catalytic oxidation of TMB by H2O2. As shown in Fig. 3 A, the catalytic activity of AuNF@FeTPPCl did not significantly change from 4°C to 90°C. Meanwhile, the activity of HRP significantly decreased and was complete loss at temperature exceeding 40°C. Fig. 3 B shows that the AuNF@FeTPPCl maintained a high catalytic activity without obvious loss at pH 1–12. The appropriate pH value for HRP is rather concentrated, and strong acids or bases can distinctly reduce the catalytic activity. AuNF@FeTPPCl also retained excellent catalytic activity upon treatment with different NaCl concentrations (0–500mM). Meanwhile, the activity of HRP significantly decreased with increasing NaCl concentration (Fig. 3 C). High temperature (> 40°C), strong acid/base, and high NaCl concentration can cause the degeneration of the protein structure, thus influencing the bioactivity of HRP. However, AuNF@FeTPPCl presented strong robustness to harsh conditions. Hence, the developed AuNF@FeTPPCl showed outstanding endurance against hostile environments. Moreover, the storage stability of the prepared AuNF@FeTPPCl and HRP was determined by preserving them in the refrigerator (4°C) for 10 months without any activity protectors. Catalytic activity was monitored monthly through catalytic TMB oxidation by H2O2. As shown in Fig. 3 D, no obvious changes were observed in the catalytic activity of AuNF@FeTPPCl after storage for 10 months. This finding indicates the excellent long-term storage stability and absence of significant leakage in AuNF@FeTPPCl during storage. However, the catalytic activity of HRP declined significantly under prolonged storage. Thus, the AuNF@FeTPPCl exhibited higher stability than HRP during storage. In brief, the proposed AuNF@FeTPPCl exhibited superior performance with respect to catalytic efficiency, environmental tolerance, and storage stability. Thus, AuNF@FeTPPCl can be used as an alternative trace label to HRP for fabricating highly sensitive colorimetric immunosensor.

To investigate the potential of AuNF@FeTPPCl as trace label for biodetection, we fabricated a competitive AuNF@FeTPPCl-based colorimetric immunosensor for FB1 detection. FB1 is a common food-borne mycotoxin generated by Fusarium species and serves as a model small molecule analyte for the development of a competitive immunosensor because of its potential carcinogenic ability to humans. The AuNF@FeTPPCl and FB1 conjugates were prepared using BSA as a bridge for the conjugation of AuNF@FeTPPCl and FB1. The resulting AuNF@FeTPPCl@BSA@FB1 was applied as enzyme label for TMB oxidation and as competitive antigens to compete with target FB1 in the samples to bind anti-FB1-mAb pre-coated in the microplate. The optimization of experimental conditions for competitive AuNF@FeTPPCl-based colorimetric immunosensor was shown in Supporting information (Fig. S5). Under the optimal conditions, the developed AuNF@FeTPPCl based immunosensor shows a very high sensitivity for FB1 naked-eye detection with a cut-off limit of 50pg/mL ( Fig. 4 A). The calibration curve for FB1 quantitative detection was constructed by plotting the B/B0 × 100% values against different FB1 concentrations, where B and B0 represent the OD652 values for FB1 standard solution and FB1 free sample, respectively. Fig. 4 B presents that the regression equation is y = −15.59ln(x) + 123.84 (R2 = 0.995), with a good linear range from 10 to 1000pg/mL, where y is the B/B0 and x is the FB1 concentration. The half-maximal inhibitory concentration (IC50) of this competitive immunosensor is 101pg/mL, which is approximately 247-folds lower than that of conventional HRP-based immunosensor (IC50 = 25ng/mL, Fig. S6). We also evaluated the reliability of the competitive immunosensor for FB1 detection by blindly analyzing twenty of FB1-spiked real corn, rice, and wheat samples containing known concentrations of FB1. As shown in Table S1, the average recovery of our proposed method ranges from 89.3% to 114% with a coefficient variation (CV) of 3.36–18.76%. These results indicate the AuNF@FeTPPCl-based immunosensor with competitive format can be used for FB1 detection in real samples, and also displays better analytical performances with lower detection sensitivity than those of conventional HRP based immunosensor.

Meanwhile, a sandwich-type AuNF@FeTPPCl-based colorimetric immunosensor for HBsAg was developed under the help of a biotin-streptavidin (SA) system. HBsAg in human serum is a potential indicator of hepatitis B viral infection, which is the leading cause of hepatic carcinoma and liver transplantation. Here, we chose HBsAg as a model large molecule analyte to develop sandwich-type immunosensor. The biotinylated detected anti-HBsAg monoclonal antibodies (biotinylated detected mAbs) were synthesized according to the manufacturer instructions. The AuNF@FeTPPCl and SA complex was prepared by covalent conjugation with EDC, and the formed AuNF@FeTPPCl@SA conjugates were used as enzyme labels to catalyze TMB oxidation, generating a blue-colored solution. The concentrations of biotinylated detected mAbs, captured mAbs, and AuNF@FeTPPCl@SA were optimized by a “checkerboard” titration method. Under optimal conditions, the sandwich-type colorimetric immunosensor was employed to detect different HBsAg concentrations. Fig. 4 C indicates our designed method can detect as low as 15.6pg/mL of HBsAg with naked eye. Meanwhile, a good correlation is observed between OD652 values and HBsAg concentrations at 0–500pg/mL (Fig. 4 D), and the detection sensitivity reaches up to 7.8pg/mL (S/N = 3) (Ye et al., 2017), which is 19.5-fold higher than that of HRP-based colorimetric immunosensor (152pg/mL; Fig. S7). Later, the robustness and reliability of the sandwich-type AuNF@FeTPPCl-based colorimetric immunosensor were estimated by analyzing HBsAg-spiked serum samples. As shown in Table S2, the average recovery of our proposed method ranges from 87% to 94% with CV of 2.23–10.14%. These results illustrate the applicability of the developed sandwich-type AuNF@FeTPPCl based colorimetric immunosensor in sensitive and accurate quantitative analysis of HBsAg.

In summary, we successfully designed thiolated amino-ligand modified AuNFs (90nm) as FeTPPCl encapsulation carrier by using hydrophobic interaction. Through the addition of ethanol, the loaded FeTPPCl can completely release from AuNF surface into solution in its free form. Although ethanol is a radical scavenger, the resultant AuNF@FeTPPCl still exhibits a high catalytic activity for H2O2 and TMB relative to HRP because of its ultrahigh loading capacity with 2.4 × 106 FeTPPCl molecules per AuNF. Besides, like other enzyme mimics, the proposed AuNF@FeTPPCl also displays strong endurance capacity against hostile environments and long-term storage stability without any leakage. Through using AuNF@FeTPPCl as a trace label for signal amplification, the detection sensitivity of conventional immunosensor was significantly improved regardless of competitive and sandwich formats. Therefore, our designed AuNF@FeTPPCl can serve as HRP mimicking analogs to develop highly sensitive colorimetric immunosensors.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by a grant from the National Basic Research Program of China (2013CB127804), Research Program of the State Key Laboratory of Food Science and Technology, Nanchang University (SKLF-ZZA-201612), and Major projects of Natural Science Foundation of Jiangxi Province (20161ACB20002).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.bios.2017.10.046.
